Long ago, when the sky was still a heavy blanket draped low over the mountains, the stars were not distant suns, but polished stones kept in a clay jar by the Old Woman of the Peak. She was the keeper of night's light, taking out one stone at a time to guide weary travelers.

One evening, a mischievous Coyote, tired of stumbling in the gloom, crept into her cave while she slept. "Why hoard the light?" he whispered, his eyes gleaming with greed. He seized the jar, intending to scatter the stones along his own hunting paths. But as he sprinted down the mountain, he tripped on a twisted root.

The jar shattered against the granite.

The stones didn't fall to the earth; they burst upward, flinging themselves against the dark fabric of the sky. The force of their escape pushed the heavens higher and higher, stretching the blanket until it became the vast dome of night we see today. The Old Woman awoke, not in anger, but in awe. She realized the light was never meant to be kept in a jar, but to be shared with the entire world. Now, Coyote howls at the moon, forever apologizing to the sky he accidentally created.

Long ago, when the sky was merely a low ceiling of grey slate, the birds grew weary of bumping their heads. The Eagle, king of the winged ones, called a council atop the highest peak, which then was only a small hill.

"We must lift the roof of the world," Eagle proclaimed. "But who has the strength?"

The Bear tried to push it up with his mighty shoulders, but the slate was cold and unyielding. The Elephant tried to lift it with her trunk, but the weight was too immense. Finally, a tiny Hummingbird fluttered forward.

"I cannot lift it," the Hummingbird chirped, "but I can stitch."

The other animals laughed, but the Hummingbird flew to the edge of the world where the slate met the sea. With a needle made of starlight and thread spun from the morning mist, she began to sew. She stitched the grey slate to the rising sun, pulling it higher and higher with every beat of her frantic wings. As she pulled, the slate stretched, thinning into a vast, translucent blue dome.

The effort exhausted her, and her wings beat so fast they became invisible. That is why today the sky is high and blue, and why the hummingbird never stops moving, forever stitching the heavens in place.

Myth: In the time before time, the sky was a sheet of heavy iron that rested uncomfortably on the peaks of the mountains. The world was dim and claustrophobic, and the creatures of the earth walked with stooped backs. The Eagle, seeing the suffering of his kin, called upon the tiny Spider, known for her unparalleled weaving.

"We need a net to catch the sun," Eagle declared, "to pull the iron roof upward with its heat."

The Spider, though small, was ambitious. She climbed to the highest crag and began to spin a web not of silk, but of captured lightning bolts. She wove a magnificent, glowing net and cast it towards the sluggish, low-hanging sun. The net held. With the help of the mighty Bear and the sturdy Elephant anchoring the lines, Spider began to winch the sun higher.

As the sun rose, its intense heat softened the iron sky, turning it into fluid blue glass. But the strain was too much for the lightning-web; it snapped, scattering sparks that became the stars. The sky remained high, cooled into a vast dome, and the Spider, forever proud, still weaves her webs in the morning dew, hoping to catch the sun once more.

Now, Coyote howls at the night, mourning the treasure that is forever out of reach, pinned against the roof of the world.

"There is no room to fly," the Hawk screeched, his sharp eyes spotting only endless green above. He longed for the updraft, for the feeling of air beneath his feathers. He watched the great Tree Spirit dozing amidst the roots, guarding the tangled ceiling.

"I will carve a path," Hawk decided. He sharpened his beak on a granite stone until it was as keen as obsidian. Launching himself upward, he began to tear at the woody ceiling. He slashed and cut with a fury born of confinement. Suddenly, the vines snapped. The canopy unraveled, falling to the earth to become the dense jungles of the equator. The vast, blue atmosphere rushed in, filling the space above. The Hawk soared into the new expanse, screeching in triumph, though he must now forever build his nest high in the trees, watching lest the green roof ever try to close again.
